A researcher tries to build a system that improves itself by generating explanations and then judging them. To keep the problem manageable, the researcher restricts each explanation to a tiny schema: about four objects, three relations, two properties. The hope is that this simplification will make both generation and evaluation tractable.

The researcher also believes that counting unusual words is probably a distraction. If relativity-like breakthroughs come mostly from rearranging familiar concepts, then lexical novelty is a poor proxy for conceptual novelty. On that view, the system should not be rewarded for producing rare vocabulary. The two strange strings in the premises therefore enter the scene as data with no established role. They are unique words, but nothing in the premises shows that uniqueness matters. So the evaluator cannot safely treat them as evidence of insight.

Now the system begins its loop. It generates compact thought experiments using ordinary concepts. The evaluator scores them. But recursive self-improvement depends on a crucial asymmetry: the judge must be steadier than the judged. If the generator changes faster or more wildly than the evaluator can reliably assess, then “improvement” becomes ungrounded. The premises say that this needed stability has not yet been explained. So even if the loop appears to converge, there is no reason to think it is converging toward better explanations rather than toward whatever quirks the current evaluator happens to favor.

The simplification to four objects, three relations, and two properties now creates a second pressure. It may help engineering, but it may also erase the asymmetries that make some explanations transformative. So the evaluator is not merely unstable in principle; it is also operating over a compressed representation that may systematically exclude the very structures that distinguish deep explanation from shallow rearrangement. A transformative idea could require an asymmetry the schema cannot represent. If so, the system will either fail to generate it or fail to recognize it.

Putting these premises together, the world described is one in which a self-improving explanatory machine can be built, but its apparent successes are hard to interpret. It cannot justify itself by lexical novelty, because rare words may not matter. It cannot justify itself by recursive evaluation, because the judge’s stability is unaccounted for. And it cannot justify itself by tractable simplification, because the simplification may remove the crucial asymmetries explanations need.

The two opaque word sequences remain in the background as a limiting case: tokens that are certainly distinctive lexically, but whose distinctiveness carries no established epistemic weight. Their presence underscores the central result. In this world, novelty in wording, confidence in self-judgment, and elegance of compression can all coexist while still failing to produce trustworthy explanatory progress.
